--aarch64-neon-syntax=<value>                                            - Choose style of NEON code to emit from AArch64 backend:
  --abort-on-max-devirt-iterations-reached                                 - Abort when the max iterations for devirtualization CGSCC repeat pass is reached
    =always                                                                -   Accept in both ISAs, emit implicit ITs in Thumb
    =thumb                                                                 -   Warn in ARM, emit implicit ITs in Thumb
  --check-functions-filter=<regex>                                         - Only emit checks for arguments of functions whose names match the given regular expression
  --compile-to=<value>                                                     - Compilation phase to run up until before emitting output.
  --debugify-func-limit=<ulong>                                            - Set max number of processed functions per pass.
  --dump-compilation-phases-to=<string>                                    - Dumps IR at the end of each compilation phase to the given directory.
  --emit-mlir-bytecode                                                     - Emit bytecode when generating compile-to or VM MLIR output.
  --emit-mlir-bytecode-version=<value>                                     - Use specified bytecode version when generating compile-to or VM MLIR output.
  --enable-gvn-hoist                                                       - Enable the GVN hoisting pass (default = off)
  --enable-gvn-sink                                                        - Enable the GVN sinking pass (default = off)
  --enable-jump-table-to-switch                                            - Enable JumpTableToSwitch pass (default = off)
  --enable-vtable-profile-use                                              - If ThinLTO and WPD is enabled and this option is true, vtable profiles will be used by ICP pass for more efficient indirect call sequence. If false, type profiles won't be used.
    =disable                                                               -   Disable the pass entirely
  --hot-cold-split                                                         - Enable hot-cold splitting pass
  --instcombine-guard-widening-window=<uint>                               - How wide an instruction window to bypass looking for another guard
  --iree-codegen-dump-tuning-specs-to=<string>                             - Dump the final tuning spec modules to the specified directory. When set to '-', prints the tuning spec to stdout.
  --iree-flow-dump-dispatch-graph                                          - Dump a dot graph for dispatches.
  --iree-flow-dump-dispatch-graph-output-file=<string>                     - Output file name for a dispatch graph dump.
  --iree-hal-link-executables                                              - Controls linking of executables. The default is to always link, however disabling linking allows inspecting serialization of each executable in isolation and will dump a single binary per executable when used in conjunction with `--iree-hal-dump-executable-binaries-to`.
  --iree-hal-preprocess-executables-with=<string>                          - Passes each hal.executable to the given command. Multiple commands may be specified and they will be executed in order. A command may either be a pass pipeline available within the IREE compiler specified as `builtin.module(...)` or a shell tool that consumes a hal.executable MLIR file on stdin and produces a modified hal.executable on stdout. Non-zero exit codes will fail compilation.
  --iree-hal-substitute-executable-configuration=<string>                  - A `executable_name=object_file.xxx` pair specifying a hal.executable symbol name that will be substituted with the configured executable file at the given path. Configured execuable paths are relative to those specified on `--iree-hal-executable-object-search-path=`. If a `.mlir` or `.mlirbc` file is specified the entire executable will be replaced with an equivalently named hal.executable in the referenced file and otherwise the executable will be externalized and link the referenced file (`.ptx`/`.spv`/etc).
  --iree-hal-substitute-executable-object=<string>                         - A `executable_name=object_file.xxx` pair specifying a hal.executable symbol name that will be substituted with the object file at the given path. Object paths are relative to those specified on `--iree-hal-executable-object-search-path=`. If a `.mlir` or `.mlirbc` file is specified the entire executable will be replaced with an equivalently named hal.executable in the referenced file and otherwise the executable will be externalized and link the referenced file (`.ptx`/`.spv`/etc).
  --iree-hal-substitute-executable-source=<string>                         - A `executable_name=object_file.xxx` pair specifying a hal.executable symbol name that will be substituted with the source object file at the given path. Source object paths are relative to those specified on `--iree-hal-executable-object-search-path=`. If a `.mlir` or `.mlirbc` file is specified the entire executable will be replaced with an equivalently named hal.executable in the referenced file and otherwise the executable will be externalized and link the referenced file (`.ptx`/`.spv`/etc).
  --iree-llvmcpu-check-linalg-vectorization                                - Runs the pass to check if all the Linalg ops are vectorized
  --iree-llvmcpu-enable-vector-contract-custom-kernels                     - Enables vector contract custom kernels for LLVMCPUMmt4dVectorLowering pass.
  --iree-llvmcpu-use-decompose-softmax-fuse                                - Enables inter-pass fusion for the DecomposeSoftmax pass.
  --iree-util-hoist-into-globals-print-constexpr-dotgraph-to=<filename>    - Prints a dot graph representing the const-expr analysis. The red nodes represent roots and the green nodes represent hoisted values.
    =mlir-text                                                             -   MLIR module file in the VM and EmitC dialects

    47→
    48→        Returns
    49→        ----------
    50→        kwargs: dict
    51→            The additional keyword arguments
    52→        """
    53→        raise NotImplementedError()
    54→
    55→    def run(self, measure_inputs, build_results, out_results):
    56→        """Run amd measure built programs
    57→
    58→        Parameters
    59→        ----------
    60→        measure_inputs: list[measure.MeasureInput]
    61→            Input
    62→        build_results: Queue[tuple[measure.MeasureInput, builder.BuildResult]]
    63→            Input, this function will get item from(blocking)
    64→        out_results: Queue[tuple[measure.MeasureInput, measure.MeasureResult]]
    65→            Output, result will put into(block if Queue is full)
    66→        """
    67→        raise NotImplementedError()
    68→
    69→
    70→def run_on_mlir_target(
    71→    measure_input: MeasureInput,
    72→    build_result: BuildResult,
    73→    cooldown_interval
    74→):
    75→    costs = None
    76→    errno = MeasureErrorNo.NO_ERROR
    77→    elapsed = build_result.time_cost
    78→    tstamp = time.time()
    79→    try:
    80→        tic = time.time()
    81→        task = measure_input.task
    82→        assert isinstance(build_result.result, Path)
    83→        cycle = task.run(build_result.result)
    84→        costs = [cycle]
    85→        tstamp = time.time()
    86→        elapsed += (tstamp - tic)
    87→        time.sleep(cooldown_interval)
    88→    except ValueError as e:
    89→        costs = e
    90→        errno = MeasureErrorNo.WRONG_ANSWER
    91→        tstamp = time.time()
    92→    except TimeoutExpired as e:
    93→        costs = e
    94→        errno = MeasureErrorNo.RUN_TIMEOUT
    95→        tstamp = time.time()
    96→    except Exception as e:
    97→        costs = e
    98→        errno = MeasureErrorNo.RUNTIME_DEVICE
    99→        tstamp = time.time()
   100→
   101→    return (measure_input, MeasureResult(costs, errno, elapsed, tstamp))
   102→
   103→class MLIRRunner(Runner):
   104→
   105→    def __init__(
   106→        self,
   107→        timeout=10,
   108→        cooldown_interval=0.1
   109→    ):
   110→        super(MLIRRunner, self).__init__(
   111→            timeout,
   112→            1
   113→        )
   114→        self.cooldown_interval = cooldown_interval
   115→        self.executor = ProcessPoolExecutor(
   116→            max_workers=1
   117→        )
   118→
   119→    def get_build_kwargs(self):
   120→        return None
   121→
   122→    def run(self,
   123→            measure_inputs: list[MeasureInput],
   124→            build_results: Queue[tuple[MeasureInput, BuildResult]],
   125→            out_results: Queue[tuple[MeasureInput, MeasureResult]]) -> None:
   126→
   127→        finished: Queue[Future[tuple[MeasureInput, MeasureResult]]] = Queue()
   128→        def submit_to_executor():
   129→            for _ in range(len(measure_inputs)):
   130→                mi, br = build_results.get() # blocking, wait compile
   131→                if br.errno is MeasureErrorNo.NO_ERROR:
   132→                    future = self.executor.submit(run_on_mlir_target, mi, br, self.cooldown_interval)
   133→                    future.add_done_callback(finished.put)
   134→                else:
   135→                    assert(isinstance(br.result, Exception))
   136→                    mr = MeasureResult(br.result, br.errno, br.time_cost, time.time())
   137→                    future = Future()
   138→                    future.set_result((mi, mr))
   139→                    finished.put(future)
   140→
   141→        sumbit_thread = Thread(target=submit_to_executor)
   142→        sumbit_thread.start()
   143→
   144→        for _ in range(len(measure_inputs)):
   145→            mimr = finished.get().result()
   146→            out_results.put(mimr)
   147→
   148→
   149→

1→from __future__ import annotations
     2→import numpy as np
     3→from pathlib import Path
     4→import threading
     5→import time
     6→from typing import TYPE_CHECKING
     7→
     8→from auto_iree.device import Device
     9→from auto_iree.log import setup_logger, set_global_log_level, DEBUG
    10→
    11→if TYPE_CHECKING:
    12→    from numpy.typing import ArrayLike
    13→
    14→
    15→logger = setup_logger("runtime")
    16→
    17→##################################
    18→
    19→
    20→class AutoIREERuntime():
    21→    def __init__(self, exe_or_vmfb: Path, device: Device) -> None:
    22→        if not exe_or_vmfb.is_file():
    23→            raise FileNotFoundError("File not found ", str(exe_or_vmfb))
    24→        self._exe_or_vmfb = exe_or_vmfb
    25→        self._device = device
    26→        self._tmp_dir: Path = Path("swapfile")
    27→        self._swapfile: Path = self._tmp_dir / "operation"
    28→        self._in_wait: list[np.ndarray] = []
    29→        self._out_wait: list[np.ndarray] = []
    30→
    31→        self._tmp_dir.mkdir(exist_ok=True)
    32→        self.is_vmfb = self._exe_or_vmfb.suffix == ".vmfb"
    33→        self._released = False
    34→        self._bg_thread = None
    35→
    36→    def _run_background(self):
    37→        logger.debug("start listen")
    38→        ret = self._device.run_exec(self._exe_or_vmfb)
    39→        logger.debug(f"console out:\n{ret}")
    40→
    41→    def _submit_command(self, cmd: str):
    42→        with open(self._swapfile, "w") as f:
    43→            f.write(f"{cmd}\n")
    44→            logger.debug(f"submit {cmd}")
    45→
    46→    def _wait_for_completion(self):
    47→        logger.debug("wait")
    48→        while self._swapfile.exists():
    49→            if self._bg_thread and self._bg_thread.is_alive():
    50→                time.sleep(1)
    51→            else:
    52→                if not self._released:
    53→                    logger.error("wait on a device that is not exist"
    54→                                 ", omit this if you are using interactive gdb device")
    55→                return 1
    56→        return 0
    57→
    58→    def _push(self, data: ArrayLike):
    59→        file = self._tmp_dir / "push.npy"
    60→        np.save(file, data)
    61→
    62→        self._submit_command("push")
    63→        return self._wait_for_completion()
    64→
    65→    def _pop(self, buffer: np.ndarray):
    66→        if self.is_vmfb:
    67→            self._pop_vmfb(buffer)
    68→            return 0
    69→        else:
    70→            return self._pop_adx(buffer)
    71→
    72→    def _pop_adx(self, buffer: np.ndarray):
    73→        file = self._tmp_dir / "pop.npy"
    74→        np.save(file, buffer)
    75→        self._submit_command("pop")
    76→        ret = self._wait_for_completion()
    77→        if ret == 1:
    78→            return ret
    79→        output = np.load(file, mmap_mode='r')
    80→        np.copyto(buffer, output)
    81→        self._submit_command("nop")
    82→        return self._wait_for_completion()
    83→
    84→    def _pop_vmfb(self, buffer: np.ndarray):
    85→        file = self._tmp_dir / "pop.npy"
    86→        logger.debug(f"pop {file}")
    87→        output = np.load(file, mmap_mode='r')
    88→        np.copyto(buffer, output)
    89→
    90→    def bind_io(self, inputs: list[np.ndarray], outputs: list[np.ndarray]):
    91→        num_in = len(inputs)
    92→        num_out = len(outputs)
    93→        self._out_wait = outputs
    94→        self._in_wait = inputs
    95→
    96→    def run(self):
    97→        logger.info(f"run {self._exe_or_vmfb}")
    98→        if self.is_vmfb:
    99→            self._run_vmfb()
   100→        else:
   101→            self._run_adx()
   102→
   103→    def _run_adx(self):
   104→        self._bg_thread = threading.Thread(target=self._run_background, args=())
   105→        self._bg_thread.start()
   106→
   107→        self._submit_command("init")
   108→        ret = self._wait_for_completion()
   109→        if ret == 1:
   110→            return
   111→
   112→        for inp in self._in_wait:
   113→            ret = self._push(inp)
   114→            if ret == 1:
   115→                return
   116→
   117→        self._submit_command("run")
   118→        ret = self._wait_for_completion()
   119→        if ret == 1:
   120→            return
   121→
   122→        for out in self._out_wait:
   123→            ret = self._pop(out)
   124→            if ret == 1:
   125→                return
   126→
   127→        self.release()
   128→
   129→    def _run_vmfb(self):
   130→        ret = self._device.run_exec(self._exe_or_vmfb)
   131→        for out in self._out_wait:
   132→            self._pop(out)
   133→        logger.debug(f"console out:\n{ret}")
   134→
   135→    def release(self):
   136→        if not self.is_vmfb and not self._released:
   137→            self._submit_command("release")
   138→            self._released = True
   139→            self._wait_for_completion()
   140→
   141→    def enable_debug(self):
   142→        set_global_log_level(DEBUG)
   143→

OVERVIEW: IREE modular optimizer driver

Available Dialects: affine, amdgpu, arith, arm_neon, arm_sme, arm_sve, bufferization, builtin, cf, check, complex, emitc, flow, func, gpu, hal, hal_inline, hal_loader, io_parameters, iree_codegen, iree_cpu, iree_encoding, iree_gpu, iree_input, iree_linalg_ext, iree_vector_ext, linalg, llvm, math, memref, mesh, ml_program, nvgpu, pdl, pdl_interp, quant, rocdl, scf, shape, spirv, stream, tensor, transform, util, vector, vm, vmvx
USAGE: iree-opt [options] <input file>

OPTIONS:

Color Options:

  --color                                                                  - Use colors in output (default=autodetect)

General options:

  --aarch64-neon-syntax=<value>                                            - Choose style of NEON code to emit from AArch64 backend:
    =generic                                                               -   Emit generic NEON assembly
    =apple                                                                 -   Emit Apple-style NEON assembly
  --aarch64-use-aa                                                         - Enable the use of AA during codegen.
  --abort-on-max-devirt-iterations-reached                                 - Abort when the max iterations for devirtualization CGSCC repeat pass is reached
  --allow-ginsert-as-artifact                                              - Allow G_INSERT to be considered an artifact. Hack around AMDGPU test infinite loops.
  --allow-unregistered-dialect                                             - Allow operation with no registered dialects
  --arm-add-build-attributes                                               - 
  --arm-implicit-it=<value>                                                - Allow conditional instructions outside of an IT block
    =always                                                                -   Accept in both ISAs, emit implicit ITs in Thumb
    =never                                                                 -   Warn in ARM, reject in Thumb
    =arm                                                                   -   Accept in ARM, reject in Thumb
    =thumb                                                                 -   Warn in ARM, emit implicit ITs in Thumb
  --atomic-counter-update-promoted                                         - Do counter update using atomic fetch add  for promoted counters only
  --atomic-first-counter                                                   - Use atomic fetch add for first counter in a function (usually the entry counter)
  --bounds-checking-single-trap                                            - Use one trap block per function
  --cfg-hide-cold-paths=<number>                                           - Hide blocks with relative frequency below the given value
  --cfg-hide-deoptimize-paths                                              - 
  --cfg-hide-unreachable-paths                                             - 
  --check-functions-filter=<regex>                                         - Only emit checks for arguments of functions whose names match the given regular expression
  --conditional-counter-update                                             - Do conditional counter updates in single byte counters mode)
  --cost-kind=<value>                                                      - Target cost kind
    =throughput                                                            -   Reciprocal throughput
    =latency                                                               -   Instruction latency
    =code-size                                                             -   Code size
    =size-latency                                                          -   Code size and latency
  --debug-info-correlate                                                   - Use debug info to correlate profiles. (Deprecated, use -profile-correlate=debug-info)
  --debugify-func-limit=<ulong>                                            - Set max number of processed functions per pass.
  --debugify-level=<value>                                                 - Kind of debug info to add
    =locations                                                             -   Locations only
    =location+variables                                                    -   Locations and Variables
  --debugify-quiet                                                         - Suppress verbose debugify output
  --disable-auto-upgrade-debug-info                                        - Disable autoupgrade of debug info
  --disable-i2p-p2i-opt                                                    - Disables inttoptr/ptrtoint roundtrip optimization
  --do-counter-promotion                                                   - Do counter register promotion
  --dot-cfg-mssa=<file name for generated dot file>                        - file name for generated dot file
  --dump-pass-pipeline                                                     - Print the pipeline that will be run

--abort-on-max-devirt-iterations-reached                                 - Abort when the max iterations for devirtualization CGSCC repeat pass is reached
  --debugify-func-limit=<ulong>                                            - Set max number of processed functions per pass.
  --dump-pass-pipeline                                                     - Print the pipeline that will be run
  --enable-gvn-hoist                                                       - Enable the GVN hoisting pass (default = off)
  --enable-gvn-sink                                                        - Enable the GVN sinking pass (default = off)
  --enable-jump-table-to-switch                                            - Enable JumpTableToSwitch pass (default = off)
  --enable-vtable-profile-use                                              - If ThinLTO and WPD is enabled and this option is true, vtable profiles will be used by ICP pass for more efficient indirect call sequence. If false, type profiles won't be used.
    =disable                                                               -   Disable the pass entirely
  --hot-cold-split                                                         - Enable hot-cold splitting pass
  --instcombine-guard-widening-window=<uint>                               - How wide an instruction window to bypass looking for another guard
  --iree-codegen-dump-tuning-specs-to=<string>                             - Dump the final tuning spec modules to the specified directory. When set to '-', prints the tuning spec to stdout.
  Compiler passes to run
    Passes:
        --test-convergence                                                 - Test only: Fail pass on non-convergence to detect cyclic pattern
        --op-pipelines=<pass-manager>                                      - Callable operation specific optimizer pipelines (in the form of `dialect.op(pipeline)`)
      --iree-codegen-affinemin-scf-canonicalization                        -   Pass to run pass cleaning up affineMinOp after tiling and distribute.
      --iree-codegen-config-tracking-canonicalize                          -   Codegen specific canonicalization pass that tracks lowering configs
      --iree-codegen-convert-to-destination-passing-style                  -   Transforms the code to make the dispatch use destination-passing style
      --iree-codegen-decompose-boundary-pack-unpack-ops                    -   Wrapper for DecomposePackUnPackOpsPass to decompose ops at function boundaries
        --use-fusion                                                       - Whether to use the internal pass fusion logic for the exp function. See #15862.
      --iree-codegen-drop-vector-unit-dims                                 -   Pass to drop vector unit dims.
      --iree-codegen-generic-vectorization                                 -   Pass to perform vectorization on tensor/linalg ops.
      --iree-codegen-gpu-apply-tiling-level                                -   Pass to tile tensor ops based on tiling configs
      --iree-codegen-gpu-distribute                                        -   Pass to distribute scf.forall ops using upstream patterns.
      --iree-codegen-gpu-distribute-forall                                 -   Pass to distribute scf.forall ops.
      --iree-codegen-gpu-distribute-shared-memory-copy                     -   Pass to distribute shared memory copies to threads.
      --iree-codegen-gpu-infer-memory-space                                -   Pass to infer and set the memory space for all alloc_tensor ops.
      --iree-codegen-gpu-multi-buffering                                   -   Pass to do multi buffering.
      --iree-codegen-gpu-pad-operands                                      -   Pass to pad operands of ops with padding configuration provided. 
      --iree-codegen-gpu-pipelining                                        -   Pass to do software pipelining.

--compile-to=<value>                                                     - Compilation phase to run up until before emitting output.
  --emit-mlir-bytecode                                                     - Emit bytecode when generating compile-to or VM MLIR output.
  --emit-mlir-bytecode-version=<value>                                     - Use specified bytecode version when generating compile-to or VM MLIR output.
  --iree-vm-c-module-output-format=<value>                                 - Output format used to write the C module
  --mlir-output-format=<value>                                             - Output format for timing data
  --output-format=<value>                                                  - Format of compiled output
  --iree-vm-bytecode-module-output-format=<value>                          - Output format the bytecode module is written in

--compile-to=<value>                                                     - Compilation phase to run up until before emitting output.
    =start                                                                 -   Entry point to the compilation pipeline.
    =input                                                                 -   Performs input processing and lowering into core IREE input dialects (linalg/etc).
    =abi                                                                   -   Adjusts program ABI for the specified execution environment.
    =preprocessing                                                         -   Compiles up to the `preprocessing` specified
    =global-optimization                                                   -   Compiles up to global optimization.
    =dispatch-creation                                                     -   Compiles up to dispatch creation.
    =flow                                                                  -   Compiles up to the `flow` dialect.
    =stream                                                                -   Compiles up to the `stream` dialect.
    =executable-sources                                                    -   Compiles up to just before `hal.executable`s are configured, excluding codegen.
    =executable-configurations                                             -   Compiles up to just before `hal.executable`s are translated, including selection of translation strategies for codegen.
    =executable-targets                                                    -   Compiles up to translated `hal.executable`s, including codegen.
    =hal                                                                   -   Compiles up to the `hal` dialect, including codegen.
    =vm                                                                    -   Compiles up to the `vm` dialect.
    =end                                                                   -   Complete the full compilation pipeline.
  --conditional-counter-update                                             - Do conditional counter updates in single byte counters mode)
  --cost-kind=<value>                                                      - Target cost kind
    =throughput                                                            -   Reciprocal throughput
    =latency                                                               -   Instruction latency
    =code-size                                                             -   Code size
    =size-latency                                                          -   Code size and latency

For example, in f16 matmul kernels on targets with only f32 arithmetic, we have to perform each multiply-accumulate in f32, and if this flag is false, then we have to round those f32 accumulators to the nearest f16 every time, which is slow.

Given the wide support of Vulkan, this option supports a few schemes: 1) LLVM CodeGen backend style: e.g., 'gfx*' for AMD GPUs and 'sm_*' for NVIDIA GPUs; 2) architecture code name style: e.g., 'rdna3'/'valhall4'/'ampere'/'adreno' for AMD/ARM/NVIDIA/Qualcomm GPUs; 3) product name style: 'rx7900xtx'/'rtx4090' for AMD/NVIDIA GPUs.     =column-major                                                          -   Use column-major layout
--

    =text                                                                  -   display the results in text format
    =json                                                                  -   display the results in JSON format
  --mlir-pass-pipeline-crash-reproducer=<string>                           - Generate a .mlir reproducer file at the given output path if the pass manager crashes or fails
  --mlir-pass-pipeline-local-reproducer                                    - When generating a crash reproducer, attempt to generated a reproducer with the smallest pipeline.
  --mlir-pass-statistics                                                   - Display the statistics of each pass

    =list                                                                  -   display the results in a merged list sorted by pass name
    =pipeline                                                              -   display the results with a nested pipeline view
  --mlir-pretty-debuginfo                                                  - Print pretty debug info in MLIR output
  --mlir-print-debuginfo                                                   - Print debug info in MLIR output
  --mlir-print-elementsattrs-with-hex-if-larger=<long>                     - Print DenseElementsAttrs with a hex string that have more elements than the given upper limit (use -1 to disable)

## Summary: IREE Compilation and Building Infrastructure Analysis

I've thoroughly explored the AutoIREE codebase and IREE infrastructure. Here's what I found:

### Architecture Overview

**Compilation Flow (from `iree_helper.py`):**
1. `_iree_compile_mlir_to_bin()` - Primary compilation function (lines 64-109)
   - Input: MLIR file, output path, various flags
   - Invokes: `/third-party/iree-install/bin/iree-compile`
   
2. Base arguments set: `-iree-input-type=auto`, `--iree-llvmcpu-stack-allocation-limit=0x999999`, etc.

3. LLVM/RISC-V target args added: triple, CPU, ABI, vector width, and optimization flags

4. Optional transform directory for tile configs: `--iree-codegen-transform-dialect-library-dir`

**How Tile Configs Get Injected:**
1. `task.py` - `MLIRTask.instantiate()` (lines 233-270) generates transform library MLIR scripts
2. Transform scripts use IREE dialect syntax: `#iree_codegen.lowering_config<tile_sizes = [[...]]>`
3. Script is written to `transform_library/transform_library_dispatch_*.mlir`
4. Passed to compiler via `--iree-codegen-transform-dialect-library-dir` flag
5. Builder then calls `build_subgraph_vmc_obj()` which invokes compilation with transform dir

**Available Tools in `iree-install/bin/`:**
- `/home/nick/work/AutoIREE_plus_AI/third-party/iree-install/bin/iree-compile` - Main compiler
- `/home/nick/work/AutoIREE_plus_AI/third-party/iree-install/bin/iree-opt` - Optimizer/pass applicator

**Build Tools Available:**
- `/home/nick/work/AutoIREE_plus_AI/third-party/iree-build/tools/iree-opt`
- `/home/nick/work/AutoIREE_plus_AI/third-party/iree-build/llvm-project/tools/mlir/tools/mlir-opt`

### Key Source Files

1. **`python/auto_iree/iree_helper.py`** (517 lines)
   - Line 21-48: Compilation argument setup
   - Line 64-109: Core `_iree_compile_mlir_to_bin()` function
   - Line 152-185: Benchmark generation (`gen_benchmarks_vmc()`)
   - Line 188-220: VM-C object building (`build_subgraph_vmc_obj()`)
   - Base args don't include IR dumping flags

2. **`python/auto_iree/builder.py`** (180 lines)
   - MLIRBuilder parallelizes compilation using ProcessPoolExecutor
   - Line 165-179: `build()` method submits multiple configs to executor
   - Line 103-139: `_Wrapped_MLIR_BuildFunc()` - wrapped build function

3. **`python/auto_iree/task.py`** (451 lines)
   - Line 172-230: `generate_script()` - generates transform MLIR
   - Line 233-270: `instantiate()` - creates transform library directory
   - Line 274-286: `build()` - calls `build_subgraph()` with MLIR file
   - Line 326-360: `build_subgraph()` - orchestrates compilation

4. **`python/auto_iree/context.py`** (227 lines)
   - Singleton context managing paths and tools
   - Line 137-139: `get_iree_compiler()` returns path to iree-compile

5. **`python/auto_iree/utils.py`** (129 lines)
   - Line 30-76: `TilingJSON` class - parses tile config JSON files
   - `query_iteration_space()`, `query_default()` - access tile space definitions

### How to Extract Intermediate MLIR and Assembly

**Method 1: Using `--dump-compilation-phases-to` flag**
- Add to extra_args in `_iree_compile_mlir_to_bin()`
- Syntax: `--dump-compilation-phases-to=/path/to/output_dir`
- Dumps IR at end of each compilation phase to separate files
- Phases: input, abi, preprocessing, global-optimization, dispatch-creation, flow, stream, executable-sources, executable-configurations, executable-targets, hal, vm, end

**Method 2: Using `--mlir-print-ir-after-all` flag**
- Prints IR after each pass
- Can target specific passes with `--mlir-print-ir-after=<pass-name>`
- Example: `--mlir-print-ir-after=iree-codegen-generic-vectorization`

**Method 3: Using `--compile-to=<phase>` flag**
- Stops compilation at specified phase and outputs MLIR at that point
- Example: `--compile-to=executable-sources` outputs MLIR before codegen
- Combined with `--output-format=vm-asm` or `--output-format=mlir-text`

**Method 4: For Assembly Output**
- Use vm-c output format (C source with embedded assembly)
- Flag: `--output-format=vm-c` (used in line 197 of iree_helper.py)
- Also generates `kernel.o` object file with assembly

### Available Compilation Phases
```
start → input → abi → preprocessing → global-optimization → dispatch-creation
→ flow → stream → executable-sources → executable-configurations
→ executable-targets → hal → vm → end
```

### Current Compilation Pipeline (in iree_helper.py)

**For Subgraph Compilation:**
```python
_iree_compile_mlir_to_bin(
    mlir_file,
    output_path,
    build_full=False,
    transform_dir=Path("transform_library") if use_transform else None,
    extra_args=["--output-format=vm-c", "-compile-from=executable-sources"],
    timeout=300,
    tuning=True
)
```

The current code:
- Uses `-compile-from=executable-sources` to skip early passes (line 199)
- Does NOT dump intermediate MLIR or assembly
- Generates VM-C output which includes object files

### To Capture Post-GenericVectorization MLIR

**Recommended Approach:**
1. Add to `iree_helper.py` function arguments: `dump_ir_dir=None`
2. When provided, add these to extra_args:
   ```python
   if dump_ir_dir is not None:
       extra_args.append(f"--dump-compilation-phases-to={dump_ir_dir}")
       # or target specific passes:
       extra_args.append("--mlir-print-ir-after=iree-codegen-generic-vectorization")
   ```
3. This dumps all phases to directory, including post-vectorization MLIR

### Assembly/Object Files

- Current code generates `.o` files via vm-c output (iree_helper.py line 206)
- Object files at: `temp/kernel.o`
- Disassemble with: `riscv64-elf-objdump -d temp/kernel.o`
- Generated from compilation, not extracted separately

### File Paths (Absolute)

- Main compiler: `/home/nick/work/AutoIREE_plus_AI/third-party/iree-install/bin/iree-compile`
- IR optimizer: `/home/nick/work/AutoIREE_plus_AI/third-party/iree-install/bin/iree-opt`
- Core compilation logic: `/home/nick/work/AutoIREE_plus_AI/python/auto_iree/iree_helper.py`
- Transform generation: `/home/nick/work/AutoIREE_plus_AI/python/auto_iree/task.py`
- Builder: `/home/nick/work/AutoIREE_plus_AI/python/auto_iree/builder.py`
- Utilities: `/home/nick/work/AutoIREE_plus_AI/python/auto_iree/utils.py`
- Context/config: `/home/nick/work/AutoIREE_plus_AI/python/auto_iree/context.py`

input.description: Find all unique linalg op types in zoo tile configs

content.text:
Now I have everything. Here's the complete plan:

---

## Full Plan: Agentic AI Tiling Optimizer

### Phase 0: Infrastructure — Cost Model + Compilation Tools

**Goal:** Build the analytical cost model and helper functions to compile/inspect artifacts.

**Files:**
- `python/auto_iree/cost_model.py` — Analytical cost model
- `python/auto_iree/compiler_inspect.py` — Compile to MLIR/asm and extract features

**Cost model computes (no compilation needed):**
```python
def score_config(op_type, shape, tile_config, hw_params) -> (score, confidence):
    vec_util     = vector_utilization(tile, vlen, elem_bits)  # % of lanes used
    lmul_est     = estimate_lmul(tile, vlen, elem_bits)       # predicted LMUL
    reg_pressure = register_pressure(tile, lmul_est)          # accum vregs vs available
    cache_fit    = cache_working_set(tile, elem_bytes, l2_size)  # does tile fit L2?
    reuse_ratio  = reduction_reuse(tile, shape)               # K_tile/K for reductions
    return weighted_score, confidence
```

**Compiler inspection (when confidence is low):**
```python
def compile_to_vectorized_mlir(mlir_file, tile_config, hw) -> str  # returns MLIR text
def compile_to_assembly(mlir_file, tile_config, hw) -> str          # returns objdump text
def extract_asm_features(asm_text) -> dict   # vsetvli count, spills, RVV instruction density
def extract_mlir_features(mlir_text) -> dict # vector ops present, scalar fallback, transfer patterns
```

---

### Phase 1: Study — Learn Principles from Zoo

**Goal:** Produce per-op-type principle files by studying all 21 models.

**Op types to cover (from zoo):**

| Op type | Principle file |
|---|---|
| `linalg.matmul` / `linalg.batch_matmul` / `linalg.vecmat` | `matmul.md` |
| `linalg.conv_2d_nhwc_hwcf` | `conv2d.md` |
| `linalg.depthwise_conv_2d_nhwc_hwc` | `depthwise_conv.md` |
| `linalg.pooling_nhwc_max/sum` | `pooling.md` |
| `linalg.generic` (elementwise: arith.mulf, arith.addf, etc.) | `elementwise.md` |
| `linalg.generic` (complex: softmax, resize, quantize) | `complex_generic.md` |
| `linalg.fill` | `fill.md` (likely trivial, just memset) |

**Process per op type:**

```
Step 1: Data collection (no compilation)
  - Parse all tile_config.json → group dispatches by op type
  - Parse all history.log → extract (tile_config, cycles) pairs
  - Parse record.json → default_cycle vs best_cycle
  - Compute cost model scores for all configs
  - Validate: does cost model ranking match actual cycle ranking?

Step 2: Gap analysis (selective compilation, ~10-20 per op type)
  - Find outliers where cost model ≠ reality
  - Compile those cases → post-GenericVectorization MLIR + assembly
  - AI studies artifacts → identifies missed factors
  - Refine cost model weights

Step 3: Write principles
  - Distill into knowledge/principles/{op_type}.md
  - Include: core principles, decision template, confidence criteria, diagnostics
  - Write supporting evidence in knowledge/evidence/{op_type}/case_NNN.md
```

**Output structure:**
```
knowledge/
  principles/
    summary.md              # Hardware model (VLEN, LMUL, L2), cross-cutting rules
    matmul.md               # ~most complex, highest impact
    conv2d.md
    depthwise_conv.md
    pooling.md
    elementwise.md
    complex_generic.md
    fill.md
  evidence/
    matmul/
      case_001_mobilenet_v1_int8_d83.md
      case_002_resnet50_int8_d87.md
      ...
    conv2d/
      ...
```

**Hardware parameters baked into `summary.md`:**
```
VLEN = {128, 256, 512, 1024}
L2 cache = target-specific (zoo data: 8MB)
LMUL = derived from tile config:
  effective_width = innermost parallel tile
  LMUL = ceil(effective_width / (VLEN / elem_bits))
  available_vregs = 32 / LMUL
```

---

### Phase 2: Runtime — `AgenticTuner`

**Goal:** Drop-in replacement for `GATuner` that predicts tile configs using learned principles.

**File:** `python/auto_iree/agentic_tuner.py`

**Budget per subgraph:** up to 10 compiles + up to 3 runs

**Flow:**

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  1. Read subgraph MLIR + tile_config.json           │
│     → identify op type, shapes, iteration space     │
│     → load matching principle file                  │
│                                                     │
│  2. Cost model scores all valid configs in space    │
│     → rank candidates                              │
│     → assign confidence (HIGH / MEDIUM / LOW)       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                     │
│  HIGH confidence (cost model covers this pattern):  │
│    → emit top-1 config, done (0 compiles, 0 runs)  │
│                                                     │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                     │
│  MEDIUM confidence:                                 │
│    → compile top-3 candidates (3 of 10 budget)     │
│    → inspect MLIR + asm via extract_*_features()   │
│    → AI picks best based on diagnostics            │
│    → run 1 to validate (1 of 3 budget)             │
│    → if good enough → done                         │
│    → if not → refine, compile 1-2 more, run 1 more │
│                                                     │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                     │
│  LOW confidence (novel pattern):                    │
│    → compile top-5 candidates (5 of 10 budget)     │
│    → inspect all MLIR + asm                        │
│    → AI reasons about vectorization quality         │
│    → run top-2 candidates (2 of 3 budget)          │
│    → compile 1-2 refined candidates                │
│    → run 1 final if needed                         │
│    → emit best observed                            │
│                                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Integration with existing pipeline:**
```python
# iree_tuner.py --tune --algorithm agentic
if args.algorithm == "agentic":
    tuner = AgenticTuner(task)
else:
    tuner = GATuner(task)
tuner.tune(n_trial, measure_option, ...)
```

---

### Phase 3: Skills — Claude Code Integration

**Goal:** Two Claude Code skills for interactive use.

**`skills/study_zoo.md`** — Run Phase 1 analysis
```
User: /study_zoo matmul
→ Analyzes all matmul dispatches in zoo
→ Produces knowledge/principles/matmul.md
→ Reports findings
```

**`skills/optimize_subgraph.md`** — Run Phase 2 on a specific subgraph
```
User: /optimize_subgraph models/Bird/bird/benchmarks/module_main_dispatch_19_static_benchmark.mlir
→ Loads principles
→ Predicts config with confidence
→ If needed: compiles + inspects + optionally runs
→ Returns recommended tile config
```

---

### Build Order

```
Phase 0  ──→  Phase 1  ──→  Phase 2  ──→  Phase 3
  │              │              │              │
  │              │              │              └── skills/ (Claude Code UX)
  │              │              └── agentic_tuner.py (runtime)
  │              └── knowledge/ (principle files, built by AI studying zoo)
  └── cost_model.py + compiler_inspect.py (foundation)
```

**Phase 0** is pure Python infrastructure, no AI involved.
**Phase 1** is AI-intensive — the AI reads data, compiles selectively, writes principles.
**Phase 2** uses the principles at runtime with the cost model.
**Phase 3** is UX wrapper.

---

Shall I proceed with this plan? Any adjustments before I start with Phase 0?

content.text: That's the right challenge to raise. Let me look at what multi-op dispatches actually look like in the zoo.

input.prompt:
I need to understand multi-operation dispatches in AutoIREE zoo. Search through /home/nick/work/AutoIREE_plus_AI/AutoIREE_zoo/models/ tile_config.json files.

1. Find tile_config.json files that contain MORE than one operation (e.g., both "linalg.fill-0" and "linalg.matmul-1", or "linalg.fill-0" + "linalg.conv_2d_nhwc_hwcf-1" + "linalg.generic-2"). Read 3-4 complete examples.

2. For multi-op dispatches, how do the tile configs relate between ops? Do they share dimensions? Are some ops' tiles constrained by others?

3. For linalg.generic ops specifically, find examples with very different instruction_seq values (simple elementwise vs complex softmax/quantize). Read 3-4 different linalg.generic tile_config.json entries that show the variety.

4. Read the corresponding benchmark.mlir files for 2-3 of these multi-op dispatches to see how the ops connect (data flow between them).

for f in /home/nick/work/AutoIREE_plus_AI/AutoIREE_zoo/models/MobileNet_v3/mobilenetV3/benchmarks/*_tile_config.json; do
  ops=$(grep -o '"linalg\.[^"]*"' "$f" | sort | uniq | tr '\n' ',' | sed 's/,$//; s/"//g')
  if echo "$ops" | grep -q "conv"; then
    echo "$(basename "$f"): $ops"
  fi
done | head -10

  fi

for f in /home/nick/work/AutoIREE_plus_AI/AutoIREE_zoo/models/MobileNet_v3/mobilenetV3/benchmarks/*_tile_config.json; do
  if grep -q '"linalg.yield"' "$f" && ! grep -q "arith\." "$f"; then
    echo "$f (pure yield - buffer initialization)"
    break
  fi
done

    echo "$f (batch norm / clipping)"
    break

done

  if grep -q '"arith.mulf-arith.subf-linalg.yield"' "$f"; then
    echo "$f (simple multiply-subtract)"
    break
  fi
done

Now I have all the data I need. Let me compile a comprehensive report.

## Multi-Operation Dispatches in AutoIREE Zoo: Analysis Report

Based on my thorough exploration of the AutoIREE zoo MobileNet v3 models, here's what I found about multi-operation dispatches and the diversity of linalg.generic operations:

### 1. **Multi-Operation Dispatch Examples (3-4 Complete Cases)**

#### **Dispatch 8 (file: module_main_dispatch_8_static_tile_config.json)**
```json
{
  "linalg.fill-0": {
    "iteration_space": "[3136, 24]",
    "default": "[[32, 24], [1, 8], [0, 0], [0, 0]]"
  },
  "linalg.generic-1": {
    "instruction_seq": "arith.mulf-arith.addf-linalg.yield",
    "iteration_space": "[3136, 24, 64]",
    "default": "[[32, 24, 0], [1, 8, 0], [0, 0, 32], [0, 0, 0]]"
  },
  "linalg.generic-2": {
    "instruction_seq": "arith.subf-arith.mulf-arith.divf-arith.addf-linalg.yield",
    "iteration_space": "[3136, 24]",
    "default": "[[32, 24], [1, 8], [0, 0], [0, 0]]"
  }
}
```

**Data flow from MLIR:** fill (initialize 3136x24) → matmul-like generic (3136x24x64 reduction) → normalization generic (3136x24).

#### **Dispatch 11 (file: module_main_dispatch_11_static_tile_config.json)**
```json
{
  "linalg.fill-0": {
    "iteration_space": "[3136, 24]",
    "default": "[[32, 24], [1, 8], [0, 0], [0, 0]]"
  },
  "linalg.generic-1": {
    "instruction_seq": "arith.mulf-arith.addf-linalg.yield",
    "iteration_space": "[3136, 24, 72]",
    "default": "[[32, 24, 0], [1, 8, 0], [0, 0, 32], [0, 0, 0]]"
  },
  "linalg.generic-2": {
    "instruction_seq": "arith.subf-arith.mulf-arith.divf-arith.addf-arith.addf-linalg.yield",
    "iteration_space": "[3136, 24]",
    "default": "[[32, 24], [1, 8], [0, 0], [0, 0]]"
  }
}
```

**Key pattern:** fill + (matmul-like reduction generic) + (normalization/scaling generic).

#### **Dispatch 14 (file: module_main_dispatch_14_static_tile_config.json)**
```json
{
  "linalg.fill-0": {
    "iteration_space": "[72]",
    "default": "[[8], [8], [0], [0]]"
  },
  "linalg.generic-1": {
    "instruction_seq": "arith.addf-linalg.yield",
    "iteration_space": "[72, 784]",
    "default": "[[8, 0], [8, 0], [0, 1], [0, 0]]"
  },
  "linalg.generic-2": {
    "instruction_seq": "arith.divf-linalg.yield",
    "iteration_space": "[72]",
    "default": "[[8], [8], [0], [0]]"
  }
}
```

**Data flow:** fill scalar aggregation buffer → reduction (sum over 784 elements) → averaging division.

#### **Dispatch 33 (file: module_main_dispatch_33_static_tile_config.json)** - Complex Quantization
```json
{
  "linalg.fill-0": {
    "iteration_space": "[28, 28, 240]",
    "default": "[[14, 28, 30], [1, 1, 8], [0, 0, 0], [0, 0, 0]]"
  },
  "linalg.generic-1": {
    "instruction_seq": "arith.mulf-arith.addf-linalg.yield",
    "iteration_space": "[28, 28, 240, 40]",
    "default": "[[14, 28, 30, 0], [1, 1, 8, 0], [0, 0, 0, 32], [0, 0, 0, 0]]"
  },
  "linalg.generic-2": {
    "instruction_seq": "arith.subf-arith.mulf-arith.divf-arith.addf-arith.addf-arith.maximumf-arith.minimumf-arith.mulf-arith.mulf-linalg.yield",
    "iteration_space": "[28, 28, 240]",
    "default": "[[14, 28, 30], [1, 1, 8], [0, 0, 0], [0, 0, 0]]"
  }
}
```

---

### 2. **How Tile Configs Relate Between Operations**

The tile configs between operations in a dispatch exhibit **strong coupling at the parallel dimensions and explicit decoupling at reduction dimensions**:

**Coupling Patterns:**
- **Shared parallel dimensions:** The first two dimensions [M, N] are identical across all ops in dispatches 8, 11, 33:
  - fill-0 and generic-2: Both use `[32, 24]` tiling (matched)
  - generic-1: Uses `[32, 24, ...]` with the reduction dimension separately specified `[0, 0, 32]`
  
- **Why?** Sequential operations in a dispatch **share the same workgroup layout**. The parallel dimensions define how the output tensor is tiled across workgroups.

- **Reduction handling:** The middle tile dimension in the tuple handles reductions:
  - generic-1 (matmul-like): Third dimension is `[0, 0, 32]` (tile size 32 along reduction dimension)
  - generic-2 (elementwise post-norm): Third dimension is `[0, 0]` (elementwise, no reduction)

**Independent Variation:**
- **Iteration space sizes differ:** fill-0 may have smaller iteration space than the matmul-like generic-1
  - Dispatch 14: fill operates on [72], generic-1 on [72, 784], generic-2 back on [72]
- **Tiling levels:** Some ops may have fewer dimensions:
  - Dispatch 14: fill-0 is 1D, generic-1 is 2D, generic-2 is 1D (different tiling levels)
  - The tile size tuple is still 4 rows: `[[D0], [D0_tiled], [D0_reduction], [D0_exchange]]`

**Constraint relationship:** Operations are NOT tightly constrained by each other's tile configs; rather:
1. **fill** initializes the output tensor with a tile pattern
2. **matmul-like generic** performs a reduction with its own tiling independent of fill
3. **Post-processing generic** operates on the matmul output and must match the matmul's output tile pattern (parallel dims), but reduction dimensions are irrelevant

---

### 3. **Diversity of linalg.generic Operations**

I found **11 distinct instruction sequences** for linalg.generic across the zoo:

| Frequency | Instruction Sequence | Operation Type | Example |
|-----------|----------------------|-----------------|---------|
| 38x | `arith.mulf-arith.addf-linalg.yield` | Matmul-like (MAC) | Dispatch 8-1, 11-1 |
| 8x | `arith.subf-arith.mulf-arith.divf-arith.addf-arith.addf-arith.maximumf-arith.minimumf-arith.mulf-arith.mulf-linalg.yield` | Quantization (complex) | **Dispatch 33-2** |
| 8x | `arith.subf-arith.mulf-arith.divf-arith.addf-arith.addf-linalg.yield` | Batch norm / scale-shift | Dispatches 8-2, 11-2 |
| 7x | `arith.addf-linalg.yield` | Accumulation/reduce | **Dispatch 14-1** |
| 7x | `arith.addf-arith.addf-arith.maximumf-arith.minimumf-arith.mulf-arith.mulf-linalg.yield` | Batch norm + clipping | Dispatch 17 |
| 6x | `arith.divf-linalg.yield` | Pure division (averaging) | **Dispatch 14-2** |
| 6x | `arith.addf-arith.maximumf-linalg.yield` | Add + ReLU | Dispatch 15-2 |
| 5x | `arith.subf-arith.mulf-arith.divf-arith.addf-linalg.yield` | Normalization (4-op) | Dispatch 15-1 |

---

### 2. **How Tile Configs Relate Between Operations**

The tile configs between operations in a dispatch exhibit **strong coupling at the parallel dimensions and explicit decoupling at reduction dimensions**:

**Coupling Patterns:**
- **Shared parallel dimensions:** The first two dimensions [M, N] are identical across all ops in dispatches 8, 11, 33:
  - fill-0 and generic-2: Both use `[32, 24]` tiling (matched)
  - generic-1: Uses `[32, 24, ...]` with the reduction dimension separately specified `[0, 0, 32]`
  
- **Why?** Sequential operations in a dispatch **share the same workgroup layout**. The parallel dimensions define how the output tensor is tiled across workgroups.

- **Reduction handling:** The middle tile dimension in the tuple handles reductions:
  - generic-1 (matmul-like): Third dimension is `[0, 0, 32]` (tile size 32 along reduction dimension)
  - generic-2 (elementwise post-norm): Third dimension is `[0, 0]` (elementwise, no reduction)

**Independent Variation:**
- **Iteration space sizes differ:** fill-0 may have smaller iteration space than the matmul-like generic-1
  - Dispatch 14: fill operates on [72], generic-1 on [72, 784], generic-2 back on [72]
- **Tiling levels:** Some ops may have fewer dimensions:
  - Dispatch 14: fill-0 is 1D, generic-1 is 2D, generic-2 is 1D (different tiling levels)
  - The tile size tuple is still 4 rows: `[[D0], [D0_tiled], [D0_reduction], [D0_exchange]]`

**Constraint relationship:** Operations are NOT tightly constrained by each other's tile configs; rather:
1. **fill** initializes the output tensor with a tile pattern
2. **matmul-like generic** performs a reduction with its own tiling independent of fill
3. **Post-processing generic** operates on the matmul output and must match the matmul's output tile pattern (parallel dims), but reduction dimensions are irrelevant

**Key Observations:**

2. **Quantization complexity (8 occurrences):** The longest sequence (9 operations) appears only 8 times and represents sophisticated quantization with clipping:

### 4. **Data Flow in Multi-Op Dispatches (MLIR Examples)**

Honest answer: **no, the simple analytical cost model I proposed can't handle these well.** Here's why:

### Problem 1: Multi-op dispatches

A dispatch like `fill → matmul-generic → batch-norm-generic` has:
- **Shared parallel tiles** (M, N must match across ops for workgroup consistency)
- **Independent reduction tiles** (the matmul's K tile is separate)
- **Data flow dependencies** — the matmul output feeds the batch-norm, so whether the intermediate stays in registers depends on tile sizes of *both* ops together

The cost model would need to reason about **inter-op buffer residency** — does the intermediate tensor fit in registers/L1 between producer and consumer? That's hard to compute analytically without understanding the compiler's fusion decisions.

### Problem 2: `linalg.generic` diversity

`linalg.generic` covers everything from `linalg.yield` (memcopy) to 9-op quantization chains. The same analytical formula can't score:
- A simple `arith.addf` elementwise (memory-bound, tile doesn't matter much)
- A `mulf-addf` MAC reduction (compute-bound, K-tile critical)
- A complex quantization chain (many ops per element, register pressure from intermediates)

### Revised cost model design

The cost model should be **hierarchical**:

```python
def score_config(dispatch, tile_config, hw):
    # 1. Classify the dispatch pattern
    pattern = classify_dispatch(dispatch)
    #   → "fill+matmul+elementwise"
    #   → "standalone_elementwise"  
    #   → "fill+conv+activation"
    #   → "reduction+normalize"
    
    # 2. Identify the dominant op (the one tiling matters most for)
    dominant_op = find_dominant_op(dispatch)
    #   → usually the one with reduction dims (matmul, conv)
    #   → for pure elementwise dispatches, the largest iteration space
    
    # 3. Score the dominant op's tile config
    dom_score = score_dominant_op(dominant_op, tile_config, hw)
    #   → vec_util, cache_fit, reg_pressure, reduction_reuse
    
    # 4. Check inter-op compatibility
    compat_score = check_tile_compatibility(dispatch, tile_config, hw)
    #   → do parallel tiles match across ops?
    #   → does intermediate buffer fit in L1 between ops?
    
    # 5. Confidence = how well does this pattern match our zoo evidence?
    confidence = match_evidence(pattern, shapes, hw)
    
    return score, confidence
```

But honestly — for the **hard cases** (multi-op, complex generic), this analytical model gives you a rough filter at best. The real answer comes from **compiling and inspecting the MLIR/asm**, which is exactly what your budget of 10 compiles is for.

### Updated philosophy

```
Analytical cost model → fast filter, narrows 200 configs down to ~5 candidates
                      → HIGH confidence only for well-understood single-op patterns

Compile + inspect     → the real discriminator for multi-op and complex generics
                      → AI reads vectorized MLIR to verify fusion, LMUL, register use

Run on device         → final validation for the top 1-3
```

The cost model is a **cheap first pass**, not the decision maker. The AI reasoning on compiled artifacts is where the real intelligence lives. Does this framing make more sense?

message.content: That's right, your cost model doesn't have to choose the best tile but filter out the worse cases.

content.text:
Exactly. The cost model is a **pruning filter**, not a predictor. Its job is:

```
200 valid configs in search space
    │
    cost model filters out obvious bad configs:
    ├── vector lanes wasted (vec_util < 50%)
    ├── working set blows L2 cache
    ├── register pressure guarantees spills
    ├── reduction tile = 0 on large K (known bad from zoo)
    │
    ▼
~5-10 surviving candidates
    │
    AI reasons about these using principles + compiled artifacts
    │
    ▼
1 config emitted
```

This changes the cost model design — it doesn't need weighted scores or calibrated coefficients. It needs **hard constraints** with clear thresholds:

```python
def filter_config(tile_config, hw) -> bool:
    """Returns False if config is definitely bad."""
    
    # Reject: vector utilization too low
    if vec_utilization(tile, hw.vlen, elem_bits) < 0.5:
        return False
    
    # Reject: working set exceeds L2
    if working_set_bytes(tile, elem_bytes) > hw.l2_size:
        return False
    
    # Reject: guaranteed register spills
    lmul = estimate_lmul(tile, hw.vlen, elem_bits)
    if accum_vregs_needed(tile, lmul) > 32 // lmul:
        return False
    
    # Reject: untiled large reduction (known bad pattern)
    if has_reduction(op) and reduction_tile == 0 and K > 64:
        return False
    
    return True  # survives to candidate pool
```

Much simpler to build, validate, and trust. No need to rank — just survive or die.

Shall I update the plan with this revised role for the cost model and proceed to implementation?

input.content:
# Agentic AI Tiling Optimizer — Implementation Plan

## Overview

Replace the GA-based tuner (`GATuner`) with an AI-driven approach that learns tiling
principles from the AutoIREE_zoo historical data and applies them to predict optimal
tile configurations with minimal compile/run budget.

**Key constraint:** The system must be fast.
- HIGH confidence → emit 1 config instantly (0 compiles, 0 runs)
- MEDIUM/LOW confidence → up to 10 compiles (inspect MLIR/asm) + up to 3 runs

**Hardware parameters the principles must account for:**
- VLEN (128, 256, 512, 1024)
- L2 cache size (zoo data: 8MB)
- LMUL (implicitly determined by tile size — must be learned from compiled artifacts)

**Zoo data available:** 21 models, ~800+ dispatches, vlen=1024, L2=8MB.

---

## Phase 0: Infrastructure

**Goal:** Build foundation tools — cost model (pruning filter) and compilation inspection helpers.

### 0A: Cost Model (`python/auto_iree/cost_model.py`)

The cost model does NOT pick the best tile. It **filters out obviously bad configs**,
narrowing ~200 candidates down to ~5-10 survivors for the AI to reason about.

Hard constraint filters (reject if any fail):

| Filter | Condition to reject | Rationale |
|--------|---------------------|-----------|
| Vector utilization | `inner_tile % (VLEN/elem_bits) → utilization < 50%` | Wasting >50% vector lanes |
| L2 cache overflow | `working_set(M_t, N_t, K_t, elem_bytes) > L2_size` | Guaranteed cache thrashing |
| Register spills | `accum_vregs(tile, LMUL) > 32/LMUL` | More accumulators than registers |
| Untiled large reduction | `K_tile == 0 and K > 64` | Known bad pattern from zoo |
| LMUL too high | `estimated LMUL > 4` | Only 8 register groups, extreme spill risk |

Multi-op dispatch handling:
- Identify **dominant op** (the one with reduction dims, or largest iteration space)
- Apply filters to dominant op
- Check parallel tile consistency across ops in the dispatch

```python
def filter_configs(search_space, dispatch_info, hw_params) -> list[ConfigEntity]:
    """Return configs that survive all hard constraint filters."""

def classify_dispatch(tile_config_json) -> DispatchPattern:
    """Classify: fill+matmul+elementwise, standalone_elementwise, fill+conv+activation, etc."""

def find_dominant_op(tile_config_json) -> str:
    """Return the op name that tiling matters most for."""
```

### 0B: Compiler Inspection (`python/auto_iree/compiler_inspect.py`)

Helpers to compile a subgraph with a specific tile config and extract features from
the intermediate MLIR and assembly.

```python
def compile_to_vectorized_mlir(benchmark_mlir, tile_config, hw_params) -> str:
    """Compile and return post-GenericVectorization MLIR text.
    Uses: iree-compile --mlir-print-ir-after=iree-codegen-generic-vectorization
    """

def compile_to_assembly(benchmark_mlir, tile_config, hw_params) -> str:
    """Compile to object file and return objdump disassembly.
    Uses: iree-compile + riscv64-elf-objdump -d kernel.o
    """

def extract_mlir_features(mlir_text) -> dict:
    """Parse vectorized MLIR for:
    - vector op types present (vector.contract, vector.transfer_read/write)
    - scalar fallback ops (arith.mulf without vector wrapper)
    - vector widths used (→ reveals actual LMUL)
    - scf.for loop structure (tiled vs untiled reduction)
    """

def extract_asm_features(asm_text) -> dict:
    """Parse assembly for:
    - vsetvli count and configurations (e8/e16/e32, m1/m2/m4/m8)
    - RVV instruction count (vle, vse, vmacc, vfmacc, etc.)
    - stack spill count (sd/ld to sp-relative addresses)
    - total instruction count
    - code size
    """
```

---

## Phase 1: Study — Learn Principles from Zoo

**Goal:** AI agent analyzes all 21 models' historical data + compiled artifacts to
produce per-op-type principle files.

### Op types to cover

| Op type(s) | Principle file | Priority |
|------------|----------------|----------|
| `linalg.matmul`, `linalg.batch_matmul`, `linalg.vecmat` | `matmul.md` | P0 (highest cycle impact) |
| `linalg.conv_2d_nhwc_hwcf` | `conv2d.md` | P0 |
| `linalg.depthwise_conv_2d_nhwc_hwc` | `depthwise_conv.md` | P0 |
| `linalg.generic` (MAC: `mulf-addf`) | `generic_mac.md` | P0 (matmul-like) |
| `linalg.generic` (elementwise: simple arith) | `elementwise.md` | P1 |
| `linalg.generic` (complex: quantize, softmax, resize) | `complex_generic.md` | P1 |
| `linalg.pooling_nhwc_max/sum` | `pooling.md` | P2 |
| `linalg.fill` | `fill.md` | P2 (trivial) |
| Multi-op dispatch patterns | `multi_op.md` | P1 |
| Cross-cutting: VLEN/LMUL/L2 model | `summary.md` | P0 |

### Study process per op type

```
Step 1: Data collection (no compilation)
  ├── Parse all tile_config.json → group dispatches by op type
  ├── Parse all history.log → extract (config, cycles) for each dispatch
  ├── Parse record.json → default_cycle vs best_search_cycle
  ├── Run cost model filter on all configs
  └── Check: do filtered survivors consistently include the actual best config?
      If cost model would have rejected the zoo's best config → filter is too aggressive

Step 2: Compile & inspect (selective, ~10-20 cases per op type)
  ├── For each op type, pick ~5 high-impact dispatches (biggest speedup from GA)
  ├── Compile BEST config → post-Vectorization MLIR + assembly
  ├── Compile DEFAULT config → post-Vectorization MLIR + assembly
  ├── Compile a WORST surviving config → for contrast
  ├── AI studies the triplet (best vs default vs bad):
  │     - What LMUL did the compiler pick for each?
  │     - Vector utilization in practice (from vsetvli in asm)
  │     - Register spills? (stack stores in asm)
  │     - Did vectorization succeed? (vector ops in MLIR)
  │     - Loop structure (tight inner loop vs branchy?)
  └── Record findings in evidence/op_type/case_NNN.md

Step 3: Distill principles
  ├── AI reads all evidence cases for this op type
  ├── Identifies patterns: "when shape has property X, tile Y works because Z"
  ├── Writes knowledge/principles/op_type.md with:
  │     - Core principles (WHY certain tiles work, grounded in VLEN/LMUL/L2)
  │     - Decision template (given shape + hw → recommended tile)
  │     - Confidence criteria (when to trust vs when to compile-and-inspect)
  │     - Diagnostic checklist (what to look for in MLIR/asm when uncertain)
  └── Cross-validates: do the principles correctly predict best configs across zoo?
```

### Output structure

```
knowledge/
  principles/
    summary.md              # Hardware model: VLEN, LMUL, L2, cross-cutting rules
    matmul.md
    conv2d.md
    depthwise_conv.md
    generic_mac.md
    elementwise.md
    complex_generic.md
    pooling.md
    fill.md
    multi_op.md             # Patterns for multi-op dispatch tile coordination
  evidence/
    matmul/
      case_001_mobilenet_v1_int8_d83.md   # shape, configs, MLIR/asm observations
      case_002_resnet50_int8_d87.md
      ...
    conv2d/
      ...
    (one directory per op type)
```

### Principle file format

Each principle file contains:

```markdown
---
op_type: linalg.matmul
zoo_coverage: 839 dispatches across 21 models
hw_tested: vlen=1024, L2=8MB
---

# Matmul Tiling Principles

## Structure
(iteration space, parallel/reduction dims, tiling levels)

## Core Principles
(WHY certain tiles work — grounded in VLEN, LMUL, L2, register file)
(Each principle references specific evidence cases)

## Decision Template
(Given shape + hw_params → recommended tile config)
(Parameterized by VLEN, L2, not hardcoded values)

## Confidence Criteria
- HIGH: (conditions where we trust the prediction)
- MEDIUM: (conditions where we should compile and inspect)
- LOW: (conditions where this is a novel pattern)

## Diagnostics
(What to look for in post-Vectorization MLIR and assembly when confidence < HIGH)
(Specific patterns: "if you see X in asm, the tile is too large for register file")
```

---

## Phase 2: Runtime — `AgenticTuner`

**Goal:** Drop-in replacement for `GATuner` that uses learned principles.

### File: `python/auto_iree/agentic_tuner.py`

```python
class AgenticTuner(Tuner):
    """AI-driven tuner using learned tiling principles.

    Budget per subgraph:
      - Compiles (MLIR/asm inspection): up to 10
      - Device runs (cycle measurement): up to 3
    """
```

### Runtime flow per subgraph

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  1. READ: subgraph MLIR + tile_config.json                   │
│     → identify op types, shapes, iteration space             │
│     → classify dispatch pattern (single-op / multi-op)       │
│     → load matching principle file(s)                        │
│                                                              │
│  2. FILTER: cost model prunes search space                   │
│     → 200 configs → ~5-10 survivors                          │
│                                                              │
│  3. PREDICT: AI applies principles to rank survivors         │
│     → picks top candidate + assigns confidence               │
│                                                              │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  HIGH confidence:                                            │
│    → emit top-1 config                                       │
│    → done (0 compiles, 0 runs)                               │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  MEDIUM confidence:                                          │
│    → compile top-3 candidates → inspect MLIR + asm           │
│      (3 of 10 compile budget)                                │
│    → AI reads artifacts, picks best based on diagnostics     │
│    → optionally run 1 to validate                            │
│    → if good → done                                          │
│    → if not → compile 1-2 refined, run 1 more                │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  LOW confidence:                                             │
│    → compile top-5 candidates → inspect all MLIR + asm       │
│      (5 of 10 compile budget)                                │
│    → AI reasons about vectorization quality per candidate     │
│    → run top-2 candidates (2 of 3 run budget)                │
│    → compile 1-2 refined candidates if needed                │
│    → run 1 final if needed                                   │
│    → emit best observed                                      │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Integration with existing pipeline

```python
# iree_tuner.py --tune --algorithm agentic
if args.algorithm == "agentic":
    tuner = AgenticTuner(task, principles_dir="knowledge/principles/")
else:
    tuner = GATuner(task)

# AgenticTuner implements the same Tuner interface:
#   tuner.tune(n_trial, measure_option, ...)
#   tuner.best_config, tuner.best_cycle
```

### LLM integration

- Claude API via Anthropic SDK
- System prompt = loaded principle file(s) + hardware params
- User prompt = subgraph MLIR + filtered candidates + (optionally) compiled artifacts
- Single inference call for HIGH confidence, 2-4 calls for MEDIUM/LOW

---

## Phase 3: Skills — Claude Code Integration

### `skills/study_zoo.md`

Interactive skill for running Phase 1 analysis:

```
/study_zoo matmul          → analyze all matmul dispatches, produce matmul.md
/study_zoo conv2d          → analyze all conv2d dispatches
/study_zoo all             → run full Phase 1 study
```

### `skills/optimize_subgraph.md`

Interactive skill for Phase 2 runtime optimization:

```
/optimize_subgraph path/to/benchmark.mlir
  → loads principles
  → filters search space
  → predicts config (with compile/run if needed)
  → returns recommended tile config
```

---

## Build Order

```
Phase 0A: cost_model.py          (pruning filter — pure Python, no AI)
Phase 0B: compiler_inspect.py    (compile + extract features — wraps iree-compile)
Phase 1:  study zoo              (AI-intensive — produces knowledge/ directory)
Phase 2:  agentic_tuner.py       (runtime — uses principles + cost model + compiler)
Phase 3:  skills/                (Claude Code UX wrappers)
```

Dependencies:
- Phase 0A and 0B are independent, can be built in parallel
- Phase 1 depends on 0A (to validate cost model filters) and 0B (to compile artifacts)
- Phase 2 depends on Phase 1 (needs principle files) and Phase 0 (uses both tools)
- Phase 3 depends on Phase 2 (wraps the tuner)

---

## File Summary

### New files

```
python/auto_iree/
  cost_model.py              # Phase 0A: hard constraint filters
  compiler_inspect.py         # Phase 0B: compile-and-inspect helpers
  agentic_tuner.py            # Phase 2: AgenticTuner(Tuner) class

knowledge/
  principles/                 # Phase 1 output: per-op-type principle files
    summary.md
    matmul.md
    conv2d.md
    depthwise_conv.md
    generic_mac.md
    elementwise.md
    complex_generic.md
    pooling.md
    fill.md
    multi_op.md
  evidence/                   # Phase 1 output: compiled artifact observations
    matmul/
    conv2d/
    ...

skills/
  study_zoo.md                # Phase 3: Claude Code skill
  optimize_subgraph.md        # Phase 3: Claude Code skill
```

### Modified files

```
iree_tuner.py                 # Add --algorithm agentic option
python/auto_iree/config.py    # Add l2_cache_size to LLVMConfig
config/*.toml                 # Add l2_cache_size field
```

  → predicts config (with compile/run if needed)
  → returns recommended tile config
```

---

## Build Order

```
Phase 0A: cost_model.py          (pruning filter — pure Python, no AI)
Phase 0B: compiler_inspect.py    (compile + extract features — wraps iree-compile)
Phase 1:  study zoo              (AI-intensive — produces knowledge/ directory)
Phase 2:  agentic_tuner.py       (runtime — uses principles + cost model + compiler)
Phase 3:  skills/                (Claude Code UX wrappers)
```

Dependencies:
- Phase 0A and 0B are independent, can be built in parallel
- Phase 1 depends on 0A (to validate cost model filters) and 0B (to compile artifacts)
- Phase 2 depends on Phase 1 (needs principle files) and Phase 0 (uses both tools)
- Phase 3 depends on Phase 2 (wraps the tuner)

---

## File Summary

### New files

```
python/auto_iree/
  cost_model.py              # Phase 0A: hard constraint filters
  compiler_inspect.py         # Phase 0B: compile-and-inspect helpers
  agentic_tuner.py            # Phase 2: AgenticTuner(Tuner) class

knowledge/
  principles/                 # Phase 1 output: per-op-type principle files
    summary.md
    matmul.md
    conv2d.md
    depthwise_conv.md
    generic_mac.md
    elementwise.md
    complex_generic.md
    pooling.md
    fill.md
    multi_op.md
  evidence/                   # Phase 1 output: compiled artifact observations
    matmul/
    conv2d/
    ...

skills/
  study_zoo.md                # Phase 3: Claude Code skill
  optimize_subgraph.md        # Phase 3: Claude Code skill
```

### Modified files

```
iree_tuner.py                 # Add --algorithm agentic option
python/auto_iree/config.py    # Add l2_cache_size to LLVMConfig
config/*.toml                 # Add l2_cache_size field
```

content.text:
The outputs per phase:

### Phase 0: Infrastructure (code)
```
python/auto_iree/cost_model.py       → filter function: 200 configs → ~5-10 survivors
python/auto_iree/compiler_inspect.py → helpers to compile & extract MLIR/asm features
```

### Phase 1: Study (knowledge files)
```
knowledge/
  principles/
    summary.md            → hardware model (VLEN/LMUL/L2 relationships)
    matmul.md             → tiling rules + decision template + diagnostics
    conv2d.md             → ...
    depthwise_conv.md     → ...
    generic_mac.md        → ...
    elementwise.md        → ...
    complex_generic.md    → ...
    pooling.md            → ...
    fill.md               → ...
    multi_op.md           → patterns for multi-op dispatch coordination
  evidence/
    matmul/case_001.md    → "compiled dispatch_83, saw LMUL=1, 0 spills, because..."
    matmul/case_002.md    → ...
    conv2d/case_001.md    → ...
    ...
```

### Phase 2: Runtime (code)
```
python/auto_iree/agentic_tuner.py    → AgenticTuner(Tuner) class, drop-in for GATuner
```

### Phase 3: Skills (Claude Code skills)
```
skills/study_zoo.md                  → /study_zoo command
skills/optimize_subgraph.md          → /optimize_subgraph command
```

### Modified files
```
iree_tuner.py                        → --algorithm agentic flag
python/auto_iree/config.py           → l2_cache_size field added
config/*.toml                        → l2_cache_size field added
```

---

The **most important output** is the `knowledge/principles/` directory — that's the "brain" of the system. Everything else is plumbing to produce it (Phase 0-1) or consume it (Phase 2-3).

Is this what you expected, or are you thinking about a different output format?

You're right — if the AI approach is replacing the GA, not coexisting with it, there's no need for a flag. `AgenticTuner` just becomes the default tuner.

I'll remove `--algorithm agentic` from the plan. The only change to `iree_tuner.py` would be swapping `GATuner` → `AgenticTuner` where the tuner is instantiated.

Got it. No config selection either — always `config/AX45_1024.toml` (vlen=1024, 8MB L2). I'll update the plan to remove those unnecessary configurability points.

Shall I start building Phase 0 now?

message.content: why do we need python/auto_iree/agentic_tuner.py?

content.text:
Good question. The `AgenticTuner` was designed to slot into the existing `Tuner` interface for the `iree_tuner.py` pipeline — but if we're replacing the GA entirely, the whole `tune()` loop (batch configs → compile → run → update population) isn't the right abstraction anymore.

The AI approach is fundamentally different:
1. Read MLIR + principles → predict config (maybe compile/inspect) → done
2. No population, no generations, no batching

So rather than forcing this into the `Tuner(Tuner)` subclass pattern, the logic should live **inside the skills** (`/study_zoo` and `/optimize_subgraph`). The skills already have access to:
- `cost_model.py` for filtering
- `compiler_inspect.py` for compiling and reading artifacts
- The principle files for reasoning
- The Claude API for the AI inference

There's no need for a Python class that wraps an LLM call in a `tune()` method. The skill **is** the tuner.

Updated outputs:

```
Phase 0: cost_model.py, compiler_inspect.py     (infrastructure)
Phase 1: knowledge/principles/, knowledge/evidence/  (learned knowledge)
Phase 2: skills/study_zoo.md, skills/optimize_subgraph.md  (the actual tools)
```

No `agentic_tuner.py`, no changes to `iree_tuner.py`.

    19→"""
    20→Template configuration space.
    21→
    22→Each template function can be parameterized by a ConfigSpace.
    23→The space is declared when we invoke the template function with ConfigSpace.
    24→During evaluation, we pass in a ConfigEntity, which contains a specific
    25→entity in the space. This entity contains deterministic parameters.
    26→"""
    27→from __future__ import absolute_import as _abs
    28→
    29→from collections import OrderedDict
    30→from copy import deepcopy
    31→import functools
    32→import math
    33→import numpy as np

    35→

    38→logger = setup_logger("space")
    39→
    40→
    41→class TransformSpace(object):
    42→    """Base class for transform space
    43→    TransformSpace is the node in the computation graph of axes
    44→
    45→    .. note::
    46→
    47→        We can regard our schedule code as a combination of transformation of axes.
    48→        Starting from raw axes in the definition of MLIR operation, we can transform
    49→        these axes by some operators.
    50→        Each operator has some tunable parameters.
    51→        Then the tuning process is just to find good parameters of these op.
    52→
    53→    So all the combinations of the parameters of these op form our search space.
    54→
    55→    Naming convention:
    56→    We call the set of all possible values as XXXSpace. (XXX can be Tile ...)
    57→    We call a specific entity in a space as XXXEntity.
    58→    """
    59→
    60→    def __init__(self):
    61→        self.ins = []
    62→        self.num_output = 0
    63→        self.entities = []
    64→
    65→    def __len__(self):
    66→        return len(self.entities)
    67→
    68→    def __getitem__(self, index):
    69→        """Get an entity of the space by index
    70→
    71→        Parameters
    72→        ----------
    73→        index: int
    74→
    75→        Returns
    76→        -------
    77→        transform entity
    78→        """
    79→        return self.entities[index]
    80→
